description: Object-oriented C++: classes, constructors and destructors, encapsulation, the rule of three/five/zero and operator overloading, then inheritance, virtual functions, abstract classes and runtime polymorphism. Every card is answerable from the primer.
primer:
## What this set covers
Classes and how C++ does object orientation: building well-behaved types, then composing them with inheritance and polymorphism. Every card traces back to this page.

## Classes & access
- `class` and `struct` differ **only** in default access: `class` members are **private** by default, `struct` members **public**. Both can have methods, constructors and inheritance.
- Access specifiers: **`public`** (anyone), **`private`** (the class + friends only), **`protected`** (the class + derived). **Encapsulation** = private data behind a public interface.
- `this` is a pointer to the current object inside a non-static member function; `return *this;` enables **method chaining**. A trailing **`const`** on a member function (`int size() const;`) promises not to modify the object and lets it be called on const objects (**const-correctness**).
- A **`static` data member** is one copy shared by all instances (`ClassName::member`).

## Construction & lifetime
- A **constructor** (class name, no return type) runs automatically on creation to establish invariants; a class may have several overloads. The **destructor** `~T()` runs automatically at end of lifetime (scope exit / `delete`) to release resources — the home of RAII cleanup.
- The **member-initialiser list** `Ctor(...) : a(x), b(y) {}` initialises members directly (required for `const`/reference members and members lacking a default ctor; also more efficient). **Members initialise in declaration order, not list order.**
- **`explicit`** on a single-argument constructor blocks implicit conversions.

## Rules of three / five / zero
- **Rule of three**: if you need a custom destructor, copy constructor, *or* copy assignment, you almost certainly need all three (you're managing a resource).
- **Rule of five**: extend that to also consider the **move constructor** and **move assignment** for efficient moves.
- **Rule of zero**: if every member is itself RAII (`vector`, `string`, `unique_ptr`), write **none** of them — the compiler-generated versions are correct. The classic bug: the default **shallow copy** copies a raw pointer, so two objects free the same buffer → **double delete**.

## Operator overloading
- Give operators (`+`, `==`, `<<`, `[]`...) meaning for your type via `operator@` functions, so `a + b` or `std::cout << v` read naturally. Only overload where the meaning is obvious.

## Inheritance
- A **derived** class extends a **base**, reusing its members. **`public` inheritance models 'is-a'**; `private` inheritance means 'implemented-in-terms-of' (usually prefer composition).

## Polymorphism
- A **`virtual`** function called **through a base pointer or reference** dispatches at **runtime** to the most-derived override (**dynamic dispatch**); without `virtual`, calls resolve by static type. Under the hood: a hidden **vptr** points to the class's **vtable** (table of function pointers), adding one indirection per virtual call.
- A **pure virtual** `virtual void f() = 0;` has no required body and must be overridden; a class with one is **abstract** (can't be instantiated). An **interface** = an abstract base of pure virtuals + a virtual destructor.
- **`override`** tells the compiler to verify a function really overrides a base virtual (catches signature typos). **`final`** forbids further overriding (function) or inheritance (class).
- **Crucial**: a polymorphic base needs a **`virtual` destructor** — else `delete` through a base pointer runs only `~Base`, skipping `~Derived` (leak / undefined behaviour).
- **Object slicing**: copying a derived object into a **by-value base** variable drops the derived parts — handle polymorphic objects through pointers/references, never by value.
- **Overriding** (same signature, replaces a base virtual, runtime) is distinct from **overloading** (same name, different parameters, compile-time).
